title: What is Orchestration?
content: Container orchestration is the process of automating the deployment, management, scaling, and networking of containers. As applications grow to hundreds or thousands of containers, manual management becomes impossible.
insight: Orchestration is the brain that manages your container fleet.

title: Introduction to Docker Swarm
content: Docker Swarm is Docker's native orchestration tool. It's built into the Docker Engine, making it easy to turn a group of Docker hosts into a single virtual Docker host.
insight: Swarm is the 'easy mode' of container orchestration.

title: Swarm Mode Concepts
content: Swarm mode introduces 'Nodes' (managers and workers), 'Services' (the desired state), and 'Tasks' (the actual containers). It uses a declarative model to maintain the application state.
insight: Swarm turns containers into managed services.

title: Initializing a Swarm
content: You start a swarm by running 'docker swarm init' on a host. This designates the host as a Manager and provides a join token for other nodes to join as Workers.
insight: One command is all it takes to start a cluster.

title: Managers vs Workers
content: Managers handle cluster management tasks and maintain the swarm state. Workers receive and execute tasks assigned by managers. Managers are also workers by default.
insight: Managers think; Workers do.

title: The Raft Consensus Algorithm
content: Swarm managers use the Raft algorithm to maintain a consistent state across the cluster. This ensures that even if some managers fail, the cluster continues to function.
insight: Consensus is key to high availability.

title: Creating a Swarm Service
content: Instead of 'docker run', you use 'docker service create'. This tells the swarm to run a specific image and maintain a certain number of replicas across the cluster.
insight: Services define the 'what'; Swarm handles the 'how'.

title: Scaling Services
content: Scaling is as simple as 'docker service scale myservice=5'. Swarm will automatically distribute the additional tasks across available worker nodes.
insight: Scaling out is instantaneous in a Swarm.

title: Replicated vs Global Services
content: Replicated services run a specific number of tasks. Global services run exactly one task on every node in the swarm (useful for monitoring agents or logs).
insight: Global services ensure total cluster coverage.

title: Routing Mesh
content: The Swarm Routing Mesh allows every node in the swarm to accept connections on published ports for any service, even if that service isn't running on that specific node.
insight: External traffic can hit any node and reach the service.

title: Overlay Networks
content: Overlay networks create a distributed network across multiple Docker daemon hosts. This allows containers on different physical machines to communicate securely.
insight: Overlay networks hide the underlying physical complexity.

title: Service Discovery in Swarm
content: Swarm has built-in DNS that automatically assigns a DNS entry to each service. This allows services to find each other by name, regardless of where they are running.
insight: Internal service discovery works right out of the box.

title: Rolling Updates
content: Swarm allows you to update service images or configurations with zero downtime. It updates tasks one by one (or in batches) and monitors their health before proceeding.
insight: Deploy updates confidently with rolling rollouts.

title: Rollbacks
content: If an update fails, Swarm can automatically roll back to the previous version of the service. You can also manually trigger a rollback with 'docker service update --rollback'.
insight: Always have a safety net for your deployments.

title: Swarm Healthchecks
content: Swarm uses container healthchecks to decide if a task is healthy. If a container fails its healthcheck, Swarm shuts it down and starts a new one.
insight: Self-healing is a core feature of orchestration.

title: Node Maintenance (Drain)
content: To perform maintenance on a node, you can set its availability to 'drain'. Swarm will move all running tasks from that node to other available nodes.
insight: Draining nodes allows for zero-downtime hardware maintenance.

title: Quorum and High Availability
content: To survive manager failures, you should have an odd number of managers (3, 5, or 7). This ensures a majority (quorum) can always be reached to make decisions.
insight: Always use an odd number of managers for fault tolerance.

title: Stack Deployments
content: A 'Stack' is a collection of services that make up an application. You can deploy a stack using a docker-compose.yml file with 'docker stack deploy'.
insight: Stacks bring Compose-like simplicity to the Swarm.

title: Secrets in Swarm
content: Swarm secrets are more secure than Compose secrets. They are encrypted at rest and in transit, and only shared with the specific services that need them.
insight: Swarm secrets are the gold standard for Docker security.

title: Configs in Swarm
content: Like secrets, Swarm configs allow you to manage configuration files centrally and update them across the cluster without rebuilding images.
insight: Manage your cluster-wide configuration centrally.

title: Placement Constraints
content: You can use constraints to control which nodes a service can run on (e.g., 'node.role == worker' or 'node.labels.disk == ssd').
insight: Placement constraints give you fine-grained control over your cluster.

title: Placement Preferences
content: Preferences allow you to distribute tasks evenly across categories, such as 'spread across datacenter zones'.
insight: Preferences help ensure high availability across fault domains.

title: Introduction to Kubernetes
content: Kubernetes (K8s) is the industry standard for container orchestration. While more complex than Swarm, it offers more features, a larger ecosystem, and better scalability.
insight: K8s is the 'operating system' of the cloud.

title: Kubernetes vs Swarm
content: Swarm is easier to learn and integrated into Docker. K8s is more powerful, flexible, and widely supported by cloud providers (EKS, GKE, AKS).
insight: Choose Swarm for simplicity, K8s for scale and ecosystem.

title: K8s Architecture: Control Plane
content: The Kubernetes control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager) manages the cluster's state and responds to events.
insight: The control plane is the brain of Kubernetes.

title: K8s Architecture: Nodes and Pods
content: Nodes are the worker machines. Pods are the smallest deployable units in K8s, often containing a single container (but sometimes multiple closely-coupled ones).
insight: In K8s, we talk about Pods, not just Containers.

title: The 'kubectl' CLI
content: 'kubectl' is the command-line tool for interacting with the Kubernetes API. It's the equivalent of the 'docker' command for K8s clusters.
insight: Mastering 'kubectl' is the first step in learning Kubernetes.

title: Kubernetes Manifests (YAML)
content: Like Compose, K8s uses YAML files to define the desired state of the cluster. These manifests describe Pods, Deployments, Services, and more.
insight: Everything in K8s is defined by YAML manifests.

title: Deployments in K8s
content: A Deployment provides declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets. It's the most common way to run stateless applications in Kubernetes.
insight: Deployments manage the lifecycle of your Pods.

title: K8s Services and Ingress
content: Services provide a stable IP and DNS name for a set of Pods. Ingress manages external access to the services, typically via HTTP/HTTPS.
insight: Services handle internal traffic; Ingress handles external traffic.

title: Persistent Volumes in K8s
content: Kubernetes has a complex but powerful storage system using PersistentVolumes (PV) and PersistentVolumeClaims (PVC) to decouple storage from pods.
insight: Storage in K8s is designed for portability across clouds.

title: K8s Namespaces
content: Namespaces allow you to partition a single cluster into multiple virtual clusters. This is useful for multi-tenancy or separating environments (dev, staging, prod).
insight: Namespaces provide logical isolation within a cluster.

title: Helm: The K8s Package Manager
content: Helm uses 'Charts' to package Kubernetes applications. It makes it easy to find, share, and install complex applications on K8s.
insight: Helm is like 'apt' or 'npm' for Kubernetes.

title: Cloud Managed Orchestration
content: Most cloud providers offer managed K8s services (like Amazon EKS). They handle the control plane management, leaving you to focus on your applications.
insight: Managed services reduce the operational burden of K8s.

title: Serverless Containers
content: Tools like AWS Fargate or Google Cloud Run allow you to run containers without managing any servers or clusters at all. This is often called 'Serverless Containers'.
insight: Serverless containers are the ultimate abstraction.

title: Observability in Clusters
content: Monitoring and logging become harder in clusters. Tools like Prometheus (metrics) and Grafana (dashboards) are essential for seeing what's happening.
insight: You can't manage what you can't measure.

title: Service Meshes
content: A service mesh (like Istio or Linkerd) provides advanced networking features like traffic splitting, mTLS, and observability at the infrastructure layer.
insight: Service meshes solve complex microservice communication problems.

title: GitOps and CD
content: GitOps (using tools like ArgoCD or Flux) ensures that the state of your cluster always matches the configuration stored in your Git repository.
insight: Git is the single source of truth for your cluster.

title: The Future of Orchestration
content: Orchestration is moving towards more automation, better security by default, and 'edge' computing where containers run closer to users.
insight: The container ecosystem continues to evolve rapidly.

title: Summary of Orchestration
content: Whether you use Swarm, K8s, or Serverless, orchestration is what makes modern, scalable cloud applications possible.
insight: Learn the concepts, and the tools will follow.
